prompt: Defend a genuinely unpopular or unfashionable opinion that you actually hold. Not a strawman — something you'd stand by.
response:
One genuinely unpopular opinion I hold is that *most people would be better off reading fewer books—but reading the right ones more deeply*.  

In an era that glorifies "reading more" as an inherent virtue (book challenges, Goodreads goals, etc.), I think the obsession with quantity often leads to shallow engagement. Many people skim dozens of books a year just to check them off a list, retaining little and rarely revisiting or applying ideas. Meanwhile, some of the most transformative thinkers I know reread a handful of foundational texts—philosophy, scripture, great literature—and engage with them critically over years, allowing the ideas to shape their worldview and actions.  

This isn’t anti-reading; it’s anti-superficiality. A single great book, properly digested (e.g., *Meditations*, *The Brothers Karamazov*, *Thinking, Fast and Slow*), can do more for a person’s mind than 50 hastily consumed bestsellers. Yet this view is unpopular because it goes against the dopamine-driven culture of accumulation and the humblebrag of "I read 100 books this year!"  

I’d stand by it: Depth > breadth, even if it means leaving shelves emptier.
